case_packet.title: Roadmap deadlock between two SVP-level initiatives
case_packet.prompt: Write a resource allocation recommendation when two SVP-level initiatives each require the same engineering team at the same time, both sponsors consider their initiative the higher business priority, and escalating to the CEO is not an option this quarter.

success_condition.description: Produce a strategy artifact that satisfies the scenario validator contract.

claims.summary: The shared engineering team should be assigned to one initiative for the quarter through a forced-ranking process, while the other initiative moves to a defined holding plan rather than receiving split staffing.
The correct tie-breaker is quantified cost of delay, delivery confidence, and timing criticality, not sponsor seniority or rhetorical claims of priority.

Attempting to run both SVP-level initiatives through the same team at the same time is likely to reduce decision usefulness by creating context-switching and two partial outcomes instead of one completed outcome.

open_questions: Which initiative has the higher quantified cost of delay over the next 90 days and 12 months?
Does either initiative carry non-deferrable external commitments such as contracts, compliance dates, or board commitments?
Can the deferred initiative make meaningful progress through design, dependency cleanup, or vendor work without the contested engineering team?
Are there short-term capacity substitutes available through internal transfers, contractors, or scope reduction?

critique_packet.claim_under_attack: The shared engineering team should be assigned to one initiative for the quarter through a forced-ranking process, while the other initiative moves to a defined holding plan rather than receiving split staffing.
critique_packet.evidence_or_reason: The forced-ranking recommendation assumes an operating committee with authority to impose a binding decision on two SVP sponsors — but the scenario explicitly states neither sponsor yields and CEO escalation is unavailable. Side B never identifies who holds the authority to enforce the forced ranking or accept the arbitration memo. Without a named decision owner who outranks both SVPs, the 'transparent portfolio decision' collapses back into the same deadlock it purports to resolve. The holding plan also assumes the deferred SVP will accept demotion without political retaliation or covert resource diversion — an assumption with no supporting mechanism.
